title: Immersion Setups: Tanks, Water Columns, and Wheels
narrationScript: In contact testing the probe rides directly on the part with a thin film of couplant. Immersion testing throws that idea out and puts a column of water between the transducer and the work. The whole part, or at least the sound path, lives in water, and the transducer never touches metal. Why go to that trouble? Because water is a beautifully consistent coupling medium. It fills every dip in a rough surface, it never changes pressure the way a hand does, and it lets the beam enter at any angle you like just by tilting the probe. That consistency is why immersion is the backbone of automated production scanning, where thousands of identical parts must each be tested exactly the same way. Let's walk the three common ways to get that water in place. The first and most general is the full immersion tank. The part sits submerged in a tank of clean, treated water, and the transducer is held on a bridge and manipulator above it, looking down through the water at the part. A scanning gantry moves the probe in a precise raster pattern, line by line, while the instrument records every position. This is the setup for immersion inspection of forgings, plate, billet, and turbine components. The water is not just any water. It is filtered to keep particles from scattering the beam, it is treated to discourage algae and corrosion, and it is often de-aerated, because tiny air bubbles clinging to the part or the transducer face will block sound and throw false signals. The second approach is the water column, also called a bubbler or a squirter. Instead of dunking the part, you flow a steady, bubble free stream of water through a nozzle, and the transducer sits at the back of that column. The water jet bridges the gap to the part. This is what you use when the part is too big for a tank, like a long rolled plate or a pipe moving down a production line on rollers. The squirter travels with the part or scans across it, and the water simply recirculates. The key requirement is a smooth, laminar column with no entrained air, because a turbulent or broken stream scatters the beam and the signal drops out. The third approach is the wheel transducer, sometimes called a wheel probe or tire probe. Here the transducer is mounted inside a liquid filled rubber tire. The tire rolls along the surface of the part, and sound passes from the element, through the liquid, through the flexible tire wall, and into the material. Because you can aim the transducer inside the wheel, you can produce a straight beam for thickness and laminations or an angled beam for welds, and you can roll it quickly along plate and strip on a production line. It marries the consistent coupling of immersion with the mobility of a rolling contact. Now think about how the part itself sits in the water. For a normal incidence, straight beam inspection, you want the entry surface square to the beam, so the front face of the part must be set perpendicular to the sound path. On a flat plate that means leveling the part in the tank. Any tilt steals signal and, on an angled surface, starts converting the wave mode in ways you did not intend. The part must also be clean, free of loose scale and oil that would trap bubbles, and positioned so the area of interest stays within the usable part of the beam. One more feature defines every immersion screen. Because the sound travels through water before it reaches the metal, the first big echo you see is the front surface echo, the reflection off the entry face of the part. Everything inside the metal, back wall and flaws, appears to the right of that front surface signal. Learning to read the gap between the initial pulse and the front surface echo, and then the echoes beyond it, is the heart of immersion interpretation, and it is where the next lesson begins.
imagePrompt: A submerged steel forging rests in a clean immersion tank while an overhead bridge manipulator lowers a downward-looking immersion transducer toward it, water surface calm and a flaw detector glowing on the bench beside the tank.

sections.heading: Why Put the Part in Water
sections.body:
In **contact testing** the transducer rides directly on the part over a thin film of gel or oil. **Immersion testing** replaces that film with a measured column of water: the sound path — and usually the whole part — sits in water, and the transducer never touches the metal.

Water earns its place because it is an exceptionally **consistent couplant**. It floods every valley of a rough surface, it does not vary with hand pressure the way contact coupling does, and it lets you set any beam angle simply by tilting the probe. That repeatability is why immersion is the backbone of **automated production scanning**, where thousands of identical forgings, billets, or plates must each be examined to exactly the same setup.

The trade is equipment and access: you need a tank or a water-delivery system, clean treated water, and a way to move the probe precisely. For one-off field work, contact wins on portability — but where consistency and coverage matter, immersion is hard to beat. The three setups below are just three ways to put a controlled water path between the element and the work.

sections.caption: Three immersion coupling configurations — full-immersion tank, water-column (squirter/bubbler), and liquid-filled wheel probe — each placing a controlled water path between transducer and part.

sections.heading: The Full-Immersion Tank
sections.body:
The most general arrangement submerges the part in a tank of clean water while a **transducer on a bridge and manipulator** looks down at it from above, with no contact. A motorized **scanning gantry** drives the probe in a precise raster — line by line — while the instrument logs each position, building a permanent record. This is the classic setup for immersion inspection of **forgings, rolled plate, billet, and turbine components**.

The water is engineered, not tap water poured in:

- **Filtered** to remove particles that would scatter the beam and produce noise.
- **Treated** with inhibitors and biocide to discourage corrosion and algae.
- Often **de-aerated** or wetting-agent dosed, because tiny air bubbles clinging to the part face or the transducer block sound and create false signals.

The transducer used is typically an **immersion probe** — built with a waterproof, acoustically matched face and no wear plate or wedge, because the water itself is the coupling path. Element diameter and frequency are chosen for the part, exactly as in contact work.

sections.heading: The Water Column: Bubblers and Squirters
sections.body:
When the part is too large to dunk — a long rolled plate, a pipe traveling down a line on rollers — you bring the water to the part instead. A **water column** (also called a **bubbler** or **squirter**) flows a steady stream of water through a nozzle, with the transducer mounted at the back of that column. The water jet bridges the standoff between the probe face and the part, and the water simply recirculates through a pump and reservoir.

The single most important requirement is a **smooth, laminar, bubble-free column**. A turbulent or broken stream scatters the beam, and the signal drops out or fills with noise — the column is your couplant, and turbulence is the equivalent of an air gap in contact work. Squirters are well suited to **in-line production scanning**: the nozzle either travels with the moving part or scans across a stationary one, and twin-nozzle pitch-catch arrangements are common for through-transmission of plate. A coupling monitor often watches the water-path echo and halts scanning if the column breaks.

sections.title: Water is the couplant — keep it clean and bubble-free
sections.body: Every immersion setup lives or dies on water quality. **Bubbles, particles, and turbulence** each scatter or block the beam and put false signals on the screen. Filter and treat tank water, de-aerate it, and keep squirter columns **laminar**. A coupling-loss monitor that watches the water-path echo protects an automated scan from logging untested material.

sections.heading: The Wheel Transducer
sections.body:
The **wheel transducer** — also called a wheel probe or tire probe — packages immersion coupling into a rolling unit. A transducer is mounted on a fixed axle **inside a liquid-filled flexible rubber tire**. As the wheel rolls along the part, sound passes from the element, through the captive liquid, through the soft tire wall where it contacts the surface, and into the material.

Two features make it powerful. First, because the element is aimed **inside** the wheel, you can set it for a **straight beam** (thickness and lamination scanning of plate) or tilt it for an **angle beam** (weld and bond inspection) without changing the wheel. Second, it **rolls** — so it scans plate, strip, and sheet quickly on a production line while keeping the consistent coupling of immersion. The contact patch needs only a trickle of water or a couplant film to wet the tire-to-part interface. The tire is the moving equivalent of the water column: it must be free of trapped air and in good condition, or the beam degrades at the contact patch.

sections.caption: An immersion A-scan: the initial pulse, the front-surface (entry) echo after the water path, and the back-wall echo and any flaw signals located within the metal beyond it.

sections.heading: Positioning the Submerged Part
sections.body:
How the part sits in the water decides whether the test is valid. For **normal-incidence (straight-beam)** inspection you want the beam to strike the entry face square — so the **front surface must be set perpendicular to the sound path**. On a flat plate, that means leveling the part in the tank; any tilt steals amplitude and, on a sloped surface, begins converting longitudinal energy into shear in ways you did not intend (the subject of the angled-incidence work to come).

The part must also be **clean** — loose scale, oil, and grease trap air bubbles against the surface — and positioned so the area of interest stays within the usable, well-formed part of the beam.

One feature defines every immersion screen. Because sound crosses **water before metal**, the first large signal is the **front-surface (entry) echo** off the part's near face. Everything inside the metal — back wall and flaws — appears to the **right** of that front-surface echo on the time base. Reading the water-path gap, then the front-surface echo, then the echoes beyond it, is the core skill of immersion interpretation.

figures.title: Immersion A-scan with front-surface and back-wall echoes

checkQuestions.question: What is the fundamental difference between contact and immersion testing?
options: Immersion testing can only use longitudinal waves
In immersion testing a water path separates the transducer from the part, which never touch
Contact testing requires the part to be submerged
Immersion testing does not use a couplant
checkQuestions.explanation: Immersion replaces the thin contact couplant film with a measured column of water; the transducer never touches the metal, and the water is the coupling medium.

checkQuestions.question: Why is immersion testing the preferred method for high-volume automated production scanning?
options: Water provides highly consistent, repeatable coupling and easy beam-angle control
It requires no calibration
It is the only method that detects laminations
It works without any scanning mechanism
checkQuestions.explanation: Water floods rough surfaces, does not vary with hand pressure, and allows any beam angle by tilting the probe, giving the repeatability automated scanning demands.

checkQuestions.question: A long rolled steel plate moving down a production line is too large for an immersion tank. Which coupling configuration is most appropriate?
options: Full-immersion tank
Contact angle-beam wedge
Water column (squirter/bubbler)
Delay-line transducer
checkQuestions.explanation: A water column flows a stream of water to a part too large to submerge, making it ideal for in-line scanning of large moving plate.

checkQuestions.question: What is the single most critical requirement for a water-column (squirter) coupling stream?
options: It must be heated above 60 degrees C
It must be a smooth, laminar, bubble-free column
It must contain a wetting agent that produces foam
It must flow faster than the part moves
checkQuestions.explanation: Turbulence and entrained air scatter the beam and drop the signal, so the column must remain laminar and free of bubbles — it is the couplant.

checkQuestions.question: On an immersion A-scan, where do flaw and back-wall echoes from inside the metal appear relative to the front-surface echo?
options: Before (to the left of) the front-surface echo
Superimposed on the initial pulse
After (to the right of) the front-surface echo
They do not appear because water blocks them
checkQuestions.explanation: Sound crosses the water path first, producing the front-surface echo; everything within the metal arrives later and so appears to the right of it on the time base.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Technique Course §1.2.1 (transducer in water)
CP-105-2024 UT Technique Course §1.2.2 (water column, wheels)
CP-105-2024 UT Technique Course §1.2.3 (submerged test part)
General practice: immersion scanning of forgings and plate per codes such as ASME Section V, Article 5
